original_hypothesis.title: StakingToken.deposit() updates accounting before transferFrom, enabling balance/availableRewards inflation if stakingToken is non-standard (reentrant or returns true but transfers less)
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: StakingToken.deposit() increments `balance` and `availableRewards` before calling SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom(). If the stakingToken is a non-standard ERC20 that can re-enter (e.g., ERC777 hooks or malicious token) or otherwise cause the transfer to behave unexpectedly (e.g., transfer less than amount but still returns success through crafted return data), internal accounting may temporarily or permanently diverge from actual token balances, potentially violating staking solvency invariants and allowing over-allocation of rewards.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Project docs explicitly state the system is expected to interact with 'weird' ERC20s. While SafeTransferLib checks return values, it cannot guarantee the token transferred the full amount (it only checks call success/return data). Updating accounting before the transfer magnifies risk if the token is adversarial or has callbacks.
deployment_assumptions: A stakingToken instance is configured to use an ERC20 with non-standard semantics (rebasing, fee-on-transfer, callback hooks, or malicious behavior)
Attackers can call deposit() with such a token approved
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: StakingToken.stakingToken is set during initialize() without on-chain enforcement of standard ERC20 behavior; docs mention weird tokens. deposit() performs effects before interaction.

proof_of_signal.lines: deposit(): `balance = balance + amount; availableRewards = availableRewards + amount;` then `SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom(...)`
proof_of_signal.pattern: effects-before-interactions; ERC20 transferFrom cannot be assumed to transfer exact amount

preconditions: stakingToken behaves non-standardly such that actual received amount < `amount` or triggers re-entrancy/callback side effects
Protocol uses internal `balance/availableRewards` for reward allocation and withdrawals (it does in StakingBase)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: accounting updated before verifying actual token receipt
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: insolvency: contract believes it has more rewards than real token balance, leading to later withdrawal failures
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no pre/post balance delta check (balanceOf) to assert actual receipt; no nonReentrant on deposit

analysis_questions: Are stakingToken addresses constrained to known-good ERC20s in production (e.g., via StakingVerifier token check)?
Can deposit() be called with fee-on-transfer tokens that transfer less but still return success?
Can ERC777-style callbacks re-enter checkpoint/unstake/claim flows to exploit temporary inflated availableRewards?
validation_requirements: Test with fee-on-transfer token where transferFrom moves (amount - fee) and returns true; observe accounting divergence
Test with malicious token that re-enters during transferFrom (if possible) and calls checkpoint/claim/unstake
Add invariant tests for balanceOf(stakingToken) >= balance and for INV-ECON-001

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deposits using a fee-on-transfer token: internal availableRewards increases by `amount`, but contract receives less. Later, during checkpoint reward allocation, rewards are computed based on inflated availableRewards, and withdrawals may fail or allocate unbacked rewards, causing DoS or economic inconsistencies.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: High: staking contract insolvency or inability to pay promised rewards; possible permanent reward loss/DoS
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All stakers in that staking instance

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests for fee-on-transfer/rebasing/erc777-like tokens against deposit accounting

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: low (small deposits can cause accounting drift)
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: single transaction for accounting drift; multi-epoch to realize failures
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: compare internal `balance` vs ERC20.balanceOf(this) over time

validation_result.mechanism: effects-before-interactions with no reentrancy guard or balance verification
validation_result.impact_summary: StakingToken.deposit() updates balance and availableRewards before the external safeTransferFrom call, with no reentrancy guard and no actual-receipt verification. With fee-on-transfer tokens, accounting permanently inflates; with reentrant tokens, checkpoint/claim/deposit can be re-entered with inflated state.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The StakingToken.deposit() function at StakingToken.sol increments `balance` and `availableRewards` by the parameter `amount` BEFORE calling SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom(). This is a clear Checks-Effects-Interactions (CEI) violation. SafeTransferLib only checks that the call succeeded and returned true (or no data) — it does NOT verify the actual number of tokens received by the contract. For fee-on-transfer tokens, the contract receives fewer tokens than `amount` but believes it has the full amount, causing permanent insolvency. For tokens with callbacks (e.g., ERC777), the sender can re-enter during the transferFrom callback. There is no reentrancy guard on deposit() — StakingBase has no _locked variable or nonReentrant modifier. The stakingToken address is set during initialize() without on-chain enforcement of standard ERC20 behavior. While the StakingVerifier optionally checks that the token is OLAS, this check is (a) optional, (b) can be bypassed by setting verifier to zero, and (c) does not apply to instances deployed outside the factory.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: StakingToken.sol deposit() clearly shows state updates (balance, availableRewards) before the external call (safeTransferFrom). No reentrancy guard exists in StakingBase or StakingToken. SafeTransferLib does not verify actual received amount. The invariant INV-ECON-001 (availableRewards + allocated rewards <= balance) can be violated when actual token balance < balance state variable.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: State variables `balance` and `availableRewards` are updated BEFORE the external safeTransferFrom call. If the token is fee-on-transfer, the contract receives less than `amount` but records the full `amount`. If the token has callbacks (ERC777), re-entrancy into checkpoint/claim/deposit is possible with inflated availableRewards. No reentrancy guard exists.

SafeTransferLib checks call success and return value (true or empty), but does NOT verify how many tokens were actually transferred. A fee-on-transfer token can return true while transferring less than `amount`.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls deposit(amount) on StakingToken proxy
2. balance = balance + amount (STATE UPDATE BEFORE INTERACTION)
3. availableRewards = availableRewards + amount (STATE UPDATE BEFORE INTERACTION)
5. If fee-on-transfer: contract receives (amount - fee) but recorded full amount → permanent accounting divergence
6. If reentrant token: during step 4, callback can re-enter checkpoint() or claim() which reads inflated availableRewards
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: deposit() writes balance and availableRewards with the full `amount` parameter before SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom() makes the external call to the token contract. SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom() only verifies the call succeeded and returned true/empty — it does not verify the delta of balanceOf(address(this)). If the token transfers less than `amount` (fee-on-transfer) or triggers a callback (ERC777), the internal accounting diverges from the actual token balance. This directly violates INV-ECON-001: availableRewards + sum(rewards) <= balance, where balance is the state variable that is now higher than the actual token balance.

deployment_assumptions_checked: A stakingToken instance is configured to use an ERC20 with non-standard semantics (rebasing, fee-on-transfer, callback hooks, or malicious behavior)
Attackers can call deposit() with such a token approved
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided. StakingVerifier optionally enforces stakingToken == olas (standard ERC20), but the verifier is optional — StakingFactory.changeVerifier can set it to zero, and StakingToken instances can be deployed/initialized outside the factory. The initialize() function accepts any address as _stakingToken without validation.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Scenario 1 (Fee-on-transfer): Attacker configures a StakingToken instance with a 1% fee-on-transfer token. Calls deposit(1000). Internal balance increases by 1000, but contract receives 990 tokens. Over time, checkpoint() allocates rewards based on availableRewards=1000, but only 990 tokens exist. Last claimant/unstaker cannot withdraw. Scenario 2 (Reentrancy): Token with ERC777-style hooks. During transferFrom callback in deposit(), attacker re-enters checkpoint() which reads inflated availableRewards and allocates excess rewards. No reentrancy guard prevents this.
validation_result.impact_assessment: If exploited with fee-on-transfer tokens: progressive insolvency where the contract's internal balance exceeds actual token holdings. Late unstakers/claimers will be unable to withdraw their rewards, causing permanent loss. With reentrant tokens: potential to manipulate reward allocation in checkpoint() during the callback, extracting rewards not backed by actual deposits. Severity is HIGH when non-standard tokens are used, but LOW if only OLAS (standard ERC20) is deployed.
recommended_mitigations: Reorder deposit() to follow CEI: perform safeTransferFrom before updating balance and availableRewards
Add a balance delta check: record balanceOf(address(this)) before and after the transfer, use the actual delta for accounting
Add a reentrancy guard (_locked check) to the deposit() function
Consider requiring stakingToken to be explicitly verified as a standard ERC20 during initialize()
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a fee-on-transfer ERC20 mock (transfers amount-1% on every transfer). 2. Deploy StakingToken instance via factory with no verifier, using the fee-on-transfer token as stakingToken. 3. Initialize with valid staking params. 4. Call deposit(1000e18). 5. Assert that balance state variable == 1000e18 but IERC20(stakingToken).balanceOf(stakingContract) == 990e18. 6. Stake a service, trigger checkpoint, accumulate rewards. 7. Observe that _withdraw reverts for the last claimant due to insufficient actual token balance.
uncertainties: In production, if only OLAS (standard ERC20) is used as stakingToken, this defect has no practical impact
Whether the StakingVerifier is always enforced in production deployments is unverifiable without deployment scripts
ERC777-style reentrancy through safeTransferFrom depends on the token implementation supporting transfer hooks
additional_testing_needed: Unit test with fee-on-transfer token mock to confirm accounting divergence
Unit test with reentrant ERC777-style token to confirm reentrancy into checkpoint/claim during deposit
Invariant test: IERC20(stakingToken).balanceOf(address(this)) >= balance (should fail with fee-on-transfer)
Verify all production deployments use OLAS as stakingToken with StakingVerifier enforced
